title: Why Coral Bleaching Is Spreading So Fast

lesson.learn:
Coral bleaching happens when corals become stressed, usually by unusually warm water. Corals live in partnership with tiny algae that provide much of their color and a large share of their energy. When temperatures rise too high, the corals expel those algae, turning pale and struggling to survive.

The speed of bleaching has increased because ocean heat is arriving more often and lasting longer. That makes reefs more vulnerable, especially when warming combines with pollution, acidification, and repeated marine heat waves.
lesson.simpler: Corals bleach when warm water stresses them and they lose the tiny algae they depend on. Without those algae, the coral can starve.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Bleaching accelerates when ocean heat and stress events happen more often, giving corals less time to recover.
lesson.deeperDive:
Bleaching is not just a color change. It is a breakdown in a partnership between coral and symbiotic algae. Those algae, called zooxanthellae, help feed the coral through photosynthesis. If the stress lasts too long, the coral can die; if conditions improve, some corals recover.

What worries scientists is the frequency of modern bleaching events. Reefs that used to have years between major heat spikes may now face repeated episodes before they fully rebound. That reduces biodiversity, weakens coastal protection, and threatens fisheries and tourism. Bleaching is therefore both an ecological and an economic warning sign.
lesson.funFact: Bleached coral is still alive at first. It often dies later if the heat keeps coming.
references: NOAA Coral Reef Watch - bleaching basics
WWF - coral reefs and climate stress

places: The Great Barrier Reef - major coral system under stress

years: 1998 - a major global bleaching event occurs
2016 - the Great Barrier Reef suffers severe loss
2020s - repeated marine heat waves intensify risk
concepts: Symbiosis - two organisms living in a mutually helpful relationship
Marine heatwave - prolonged unusually warm ocean water
Ocean acidification - seawater becoming less alkaline
Resilience - the ability to recover after stress
